findings.description: Reflected XSS: request URI (consoleRoot) used in HTML href/src attributes without encoding in WebConsole plugin
findings.reasoning: The variable `consoleRoot` is derived directly from `request.getRequestURI()` (line 102-103): `String consoleRoot = request.getRequestURI().substring(0, request.getRequestURI().indexOf(pluginResource));`. This user-controlled value is inserted into an HTML anchor's href attribute via `String.format()` without any HTML encoding or URL validation. An attacker can craft a request URI containing HTML-breaking characters or script payloads. Since this is in the Felix Web Console plugin (accessed by admins), the attack scenario requires admin access but represents stored risk of malicious link injection. The content type is text/html (default for HttpServlet response). No escaping is applied between the source (`request.getRequestURI()`) and the sink (`printWriter.println(String.format(...))`). Similarly, lines 117-118 use `consoleRoot` in printf with LINK_TAG and SCRIPT_TAG patterns for CSS/JS resources without encoding.

evidence.dataflow_analysis:
Source: request.getRequestURI() at line 102
Flow: consoleRoot = requestURI.substring(0, requestURI.indexOf(pluginResource))
Sink: String.format("<li id='config-tab'><a href='%s'>...", consoleRoot + URI_CONFIG_XHR) at line 123-125
No sanitization between source and sink.
evidence.notes: The Felix Web Console is typically admin-only, reducing exploitability somewhat. However the URI parsing allows arbitrary prefix injection. An attacker who can trick an admin into clicking a specially crafted URL can execute XSS.

exploit_payload.scenario: An attacker tricks an authenticated admin user into clicking a crafted URL to the Felix WebConsole XSS Protection plugin, causing JavaScript execution in the admin's browser session
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Target must be an authenticated admin user with access to the Felix Web Console

findings.description: Reflected XSS: request URI (consoleRoot) used in HTML script/link src attributes without encoding in writeAntiSamyConfiguration
findings.reasoning: The `consoleRoot` variable derived from `request.getRequestURI()` (user-controlled) is passed directly into `printWriter.printf(SCRIPT_TAG, ...)` and `printWriter.printf(LINK_TAG, ...)` in the `writeAntiSamyConfiguration` method. SCRIPT_TAG = `"<script src='%s'></script>\n"` and LINK_TAG = `"<link rel='stylesheet' type='text/css' href='%s'>"`. The `consoleRoot` is inserted as the `%s` portion of these tags without any HTML encoding, allowing injection into the `src` and `href` attributes. This response has `text/html` content type (set explicitly at line 179: `response.setContentType("text/html")`). An attacker can inject content into the script src attribute by crafting the request URI, potentially loading attacker-controlled scripts.

evidence.notes: This is triggered when the pluginResource equals URI_CONFIG_XHR (/xssprotection/config.xhr). The consoleRoot parameter flows from doGet to writeAntiSamyConfiguration without any sanitization.

exploit_payload.scenario: An attacker tricks an authenticated admin into visiting a crafted URL that injects a malicious script tag into the XSS Protection config tab of the Felix Web Console
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Target must be an authenticated admin user with access to the Felix Web Console; content-type is explicitly text/html

findings.description: Reflected XSS: request URI (consoleRoot) used unencoded in HTML link/script tag href and src attributes in main doGet HTML output
findings.code_snippet:
printWriter.printf(LINK_TAG, consoleRoot + RES_URI_XSS_CSS);
                printWriter.printf(SCRIPT_TAG, consoleRoot + RES_URI_XSS_JS);
findings.reasoning: In the main `doGet` else-branch (the default page rendered as HTML), `consoleRoot` derived from `request.getRequestURI()` is directly placed into LINK_TAG (`<link rel='stylesheet' type='text/css' href='%s'>`) and SCRIPT_TAG (`<script src='%s'></script>`). The `%s` placeholder is filled with `consoleRoot + RES_URI_XSS_CSS` and `consoleRoot + RES_URI_XSS_JS` respectively. No HTML encoding is applied to `consoleRoot`. An attacker can break out of the href/src attribute context and inject arbitrary HTML. The response Content-Type defaults to text/html for HttpServlet responses.

evidence.notes: Same source (consoleRoot from request.getRequestURI()) as finding at line 123-125, but different sinks (LINK_TAG and SCRIPT_TAG rather than an anchor href). Injection into script src is particularly dangerous as it can load attacker-controlled scripts.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker crafts a malicious URL to the WebConsole plugin main page that injects a script tag via the unencoded consoleRoot variable
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Target must be an authenticated admin user with access to the Felix Web Console

findings.description: False positive: blocked URLs in generateInvalidUrlsJSONReport are output as JSON (application/json), not HTML - no XSS risk
findings.reasoning: Although blocked URLs are stored and then output via this method, the output is properly JSON-encoded through the Jakarta JSON API (`Json.createObjectBuilder().add("href", entry.getKey())`). The JSON writer handles proper escaping of special characters. The Content-Type is set to `application/json`, which modern browsers will not render as HTML. While the content-type is technically set AFTER `response.getWriter()` is called, most servlet containers buffer the response and allow headers to be set until the first `flush()` or `write()` call on the writer - and no write has happened before `setContentType` is called here. The JsonWriter also inherently encodes the URL data as proper JSON string, preventing XSS even if somehow rendered as HTML. This is a false positive.

evidence.code_context:
Line 159: private void generateInvalidUrlsJSONReport(HttpServletResponse response) {
Line 162-167: for loop building JsonObject with entry.getKey() (URL) via Json.createObjectBuilder().add()
Line 169: try (JsonWriter writer = Json.createWriter(response.getWriter()))
Line 170: response.setContentType("application/json");
Line 171-172: writer.writeObject(...)

The URL data flows through Jakarta JSON API which properly encodes JSON strings.
Content-Type is application/json preventing browser HTML rendering.
evidence.notes: The CodeQL query appears to flag this because URL data flows to a response writer without explicit HTML encoding. However, JSON encoding is applied via the Jakarta JSON API, and the content type is application/json.

findings.description: Potential javascript:/data: URI bypass in isValidHref URL validation - SCHEME_PATTERN blocks javascript but not data: URIs
findings.reasoning:
The `SCHEME_PATTERN` in XSSFilterImpl is `(?!\s*javascript)\p{L}[\p{L}\p{N}+.\-]*` and `RELATIVE_REF` uses `(?!\s*javascript(?::|:))`. Both only explicitly block `javascript:` but not `data:` URIs. The `URI` pattern uses `SCHEME_PATTERN + ":" + HIER_PART + ...` where SCHEME_PATTERN allows any letter-starting scheme except `javascript`. This means `data:text/html,<script>alert(1)</script>` could match the URI pattern and be considered a valid href. The `getValidHref` method in XSSAPIImpl encodes special characters but `data:text/html,<script>alert(1)</script>` doesn't contain the characters being encoded (quotes, angle brackets in the scheme/content after encoding). However, the hrefAttribute may be configured from the AntiSamy policy file rather than using the DEFAULT_HREF_ATTRIBUTE, so the actual behavior depends on the deployed policy configuration. This requires manual review to confirm exploitability.", "evidence": {"code_context": "SCHEME_PATTERN = "(?!\\s*javascript)\\p{L}[\\p{L}\\p{N}+.\\-]*" - blocks javascript but not data
RELATIVE_REF = "(?!\\s*javascript(?::|:))" + RELATIVE_PART... - only blocks javascript:
URI = SCHEME_PATTERN + ":" + HIER_PART + ... - data: would match as valid scheme

runHrefValidation checks against hrefAttribute which uses either configured policy regexes or DEFAULT_HREF_ATTRIBUTE (RELATIVE_REF and URI patterns).

data:text/html,<script>alert(1)</script> - the '<' and '>' would be encoded by getValidHref in XSSAPIImpl to %3C and %3E, but data:text/html,%3Cscript%3Ealert(1)%3C/script%3E could still execute in some browsers.", "notes": "The actual hrefAttribute used in runHrefValidation depends on the AntiSamy policy loaded from config.xml. The DEFAULT_HREF_ATTRIBUTE uses RELATIVE_REF and URI patterns. Whether data: URIs are truly exploitable depends on whether browsers execute data: URIs in href attributes and whether the encoding in getValidHref prevents the injection."}

findings.description: False positive: FallbackATag is a security control that enforces restrictive href validation using simplified allowlist patterns - not a vulnerability
findings.reasoning: FallbackATag is a security control, not a vulnerability. It overrides the href attribute validation to use FALLBACK_HREF_ATTRIBUTE (ON_SITE_SIMPLIFIED and OFF_SITE_SIMPLIFIED patterns) when the primary AntiSamy policy causes StackOverflow errors. FALLBACK_HREF_ATTRIBUTE only allows relative on-site URLs matching alphanumeric/safe characters OR off-site URLs with http/https/ftp/mailto schemes. This is MORE restrictive than the default policy and explicitly prevents javascript: and data: URI injection. The CodeQL query results for this file are just observations about string literals and method calls in the class - there is no actual XSS vulnerability here.

evidence.code_context:
FALLBACK_HREF_ATTRIBUTE uses:
- ON_SITE_SIMPLIFIED: ([\p{L}\p{N}\\.#@\$%\+&;:\-_~,\?=/!\*\(\)]*|\#(\w)+)
- OFF_SITE_SIMPLIFIED: (\s)*((ht|f)tp(s?)://|mailto:)[\p{L}\p{N}]+...

These patterns only allow:
1. On-site relative URLs with safe characters (no javascript: possible)
2. Off-site URLs only with http/https/ftp/mailto schemes

This is a security fallback that is MORE restrictive, not less.
evidence.notes: Query 4 results are all structural observations (String 'a', getAction, isAction, mutateAction, etc.) - none represent actual data flows to dangerous sinks.

findings.description: False positive: String literals in getValidHref and getValidJSToken are encoding/validation constants, not unencoded user data flows to output
findings.reasoning: The Query 9 results catalog string literals used in URL validation methods (getValidHref, getValidJSToken, getValidCSSColor, getValidMultiLineComment, isValidHref, runHrefValidation). These strings are: (1) character encoding replacement targets like '%22', '%27', '%3E', '%3C', '%60', '%20' which are security encodings applied to user input, (2) error/log messages like 'Unable to validate URL.', (3) return value '' for invalid URLs, (4) regex patterns for validation. None of these represent unencoded user data flowing to HTML output. The getValidHref method actually applies URL-encoding to dangerous characters before validating, which is the correct security pattern. These CodeQL results are false positives from a query that is cataloging validation patterns rather than identifying actual vulnerabilities.

evidence.code_context:
getValidHref encodes special chars before validation:
- '"' -> '%22'
- "'" -> '%27'
- '>' -> '%3E'
- '<' -> '%3C'
- '`' -> '%60'
- ' ' -> '%20'

Then calls xssFilter.isValidHref(encodedUrl) - validation gate
Returns empty string if invalid - safe fallback

getValidJSToken uses regex allowlist '[0-9a-zA-Z_$][0-9a-zA-Z_$.]*' for identifiers
getValidCSSColor uses regex allowlist for CSS color values
evidence.notes: Query 9 results are structural observations about what string constants exist in URL validation methods. None of them represent data flows to XSS sinks.

findings.description: doGet writes HTML response without setting Content-Type header, enabling MIME sniffing and potential XSS
findings.reasoning: The else branch of doGet (lines 115-143) calls response.getWriter() at line 116 and writes HTML content to the response, but never calls response.setContentType("text/html") before writing. Without an explicit Content-Type header, the browser may default to or MIME-sniff the response. Since the class extends HttpServlet directly (not Felix's AbstractWebConsolePlugin which would handle Content-Type), there is no framework-level Content-Type management. While the content written is mostly static HTML, the `consoleRoot` value is derived from `request.getRequestURI()` (line 102-103) and used in the HTML output. If a browser MIME-sniffs the response as HTML (which it would, given the HTML content), an attacker who can influence the URL routing might be able to inject content. The primary concern is the missing Content-Type, which violates secure coding practices and enables potential MIME sniffing attacks.

evidence.code_context: The doGet method at line 99 handles multiple request paths. The 'else' branch (lines 115-143) writes HTML but never sets Content-Type. The class extends HttpServlet directly: 'public class XSSProtectionAPIWebConsolePlugin extends HttpServlet'. No setContentType call exists in the doGet else branch or before it. In contrast, other methods like writeAntiSamyConfiguration (line 179) and streamResource (line 220) properly set Content-Type before writing.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: request.getPathInfo() -> pluginResource -> none of the if/else conditions match -> else branch executes -> response.getWriter() called at line 116 -> HTML written without Content-Type being set
evidence.notes: The Felix WebConsole framework typically wraps plugin responses and may set Content-Type, but since this class extends HttpServlet directly (not AbstractWebConsolePlugin), no such wrapping occurs. The content includes user-influenced data (consoleRoot from request URI) embedded in HTML.

exploit_payload.scenario: An attacker accesses the Felix WebConsole XSS plugin endpoint with a crafted path; the HTML response is returned without Content-Type header, allowing browser MIME sniffing and potential script injection via the consoleRoot URL parameter embedded in script/link tags
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Access to the Felix WebConsole (typically requires admin authentication or network access to internal console)

findings.description: generateInvalidUrlsJSONReport calls getWriter() before setContentType(), making the Content-Type header ineffective and enabling MIME sniffing of JSON response
findings.reasoning: In the Servlet specification, calling response.getWriter() or response.getOutputStream() commits the response headers. Once committed, subsequent calls to setContentType() have no effect. Here, response.getWriter() is called at line 169 (inside the try-with-resources expression), and response.setContentType("application/json") is called at line 170 inside the try block body — but after getWriter() has already been invoked. This means the response may be served without a proper Content-Type header (or with the servlet container's default, often text/plain or text/html depending on container). If the browser MIME-sniffs the response as text/html (the JSON content contains URLs which could be crafted), XSS could occur. The response includes user-controlled URL data (entry.getKey() from statusService.getInvalidUrls()) embedded in JSON without HTML encoding, meaning if the browser treats it as HTML, the URLs could contain script payloads.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: statusService.getInvalidUrls() returns blocked URL entries -> these are embedded in JSON -> written to response.getWriter() before Content-Type is set -> browser may MIME-sniff response as text/html
evidence.notes: Per Servlet 3.x spec section 5.2: 'Calling setContentType after getWriter has been called on the response will have no effect.' This is a clear ordering bug that defeats the intent of setting application/json.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker causes malicious URLs to be stored in the blocked URLs list, then triggers the JSON report endpoint where getWriter() is called before setContentType(), causing the JSON response to potentially be MIME-sniffed as HTML and executed as script
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Access to Felix WebConsole (admin credentials), ability to trigger URL blocking in XSS filter with crafted payloads
exploit_payload.category: Cross-site Scripting via MIME sniffing of JSON response

findings.description: writeAntiSamyConfiguration writes antiSamyPolicy.getPath() to HTML response without HTML encoding
findings.reasoning: The `antiSamyPolicy.getPath()` value is written directly to the HTML response via printf without HTML encoding. If an attacker can control the path of the AntiSamy policy configuration (e.g., by setting a malicious OSGi configuration path), the path string would be reflected into HTML without escaping, enabling stored XSS. However, this requires admin access to configure the policy path, making it effectively self-XSS or requires prior admin compromise. The Content-Type is correctly set to text/html (line 179) before writing. This is a manual review item since it depends on whether getPath() can ever return attacker-controlled data.

evidence.notes: getPath() likely returns an OSGi configuration path which could be controlled by an admin. While this requires admin access, it's still a defense-in-depth concern. The path is not HTML-encoded before being written to text/html response.

findings.description: streamAntiSamyConfiguration correctly sets Content-Type before getOutputStream; also uses Content-Disposition: attachment preventing browser rendering
findings.reasoning: setContentType("application/xml") at line 150 is called BEFORE getOutputStream() at line 153. The ordering is correct. Additionally, Content-Disposition: attachment is set, which forces the browser to download rather than render the content, providing an additional layer of protection against MIME sniffing attacks. This is a false positive - the method follows correct coding practices.

evidence.code_context:
Line 150: response.setContentType("application/xml") - called before
Line 151: response.setHeader("Content-Disposition", "attachment; filename=config.xml") - download forced
Line 153: response.getOutputStream() - called after setContentType
Correct ordering: setContentType -> setHeader -> getOutputStream

findings.description: writeAntiSamyConfiguration correctly sets Content-Type text/html before calling getWriter()
findings.reasoning: setContentType("text/html") at line 179 is called BEFORE response.getWriter() at line 184. The ordering is correct per Servlet spec requirements. Content-Type is properly set before the writer is obtained. This is a false positive - the CodeQL query flagged these lines but there is no ordering violation here.

evidence.code_context:
Line 179: response.setContentType("text/html") - called first
Line 184: PrintWriter printWriter = response.getWriter() - called after
Correct ordering: setContentType -> getWriter

findings.description: streamResource correctly sets Content-Type before calling getOutputStream()
findings.reasoning: setContentType(contentType) at line 220 is called BEFORE response.getOutputStream() at line 221. The ordering is correct per Servlet spec. The contentType is passed from the caller (either "text/css" or "application/javascript") based on a fixed allowlist check. The file name is sanitized via FilenameUtils.getName() before use. This is a false positive - the CodeQL query flagged these lines but there is no ordering violation here.

evidence.code_context:
Line 220: response.setContentType(contentType) - called first
Line 221: IOUtils.copy(cssStream, response.getOutputStream()) - called after
Correct ordering: setContentType -> getOutputStream
contentType is either 'text/css' or 'application/javascript' - hardcoded safe values from caller

findings.description: getPathInfo/substring at lines 101-103 are used for routing only, not directly reflected in HTML output without encoding
findings.reasoning: The `pluginResource` from `request.getPathInfo()` is used only for routing comparisons (CSS_RESOURCES.contains(), JS_RESOURCES.contains(), URI_CONFIG_XHR.equalsIgnoreCase(), etc.) at lines 104-113. It is never directly written to the response. The `consoleRoot` derived from `request.getRequestURI()` is used in HTML output as a URL prefix in script/link tags (lines 118-121, 185-186), but only in the form of URL attribute values in trusted tag templates (SCRIPT_TAG, LINK_TAG). The primary Content-Type issue for the doGet HTML branch has already been reported separately. These specific lines (101-103) are intermediate data manipulation steps, not direct XSS sinks.

evidence.code_context:
Line 101: String pluginResource = request.getPathInfo(); - used for routing only
Line 102-103: String consoleRoot = request.getRequestURI().substring(...) - used as URL prefix in script/link tags
Lines 104-113: if/else routing using pluginResource - not reflected in response
Lines 118-121: consoleRoot used in printf with LINK_TAG/SCRIPT_TAG templates for src/href attributes

findings.description: println calls at lines 119-141 are static HTML content within the doGet HTML branch - already covered by the missing Content-Type finding at line 116
findings.reasoning: These println calls (lines 119-141) write mostly static HTML strings to the response. The only dynamic data is `consoleRoot` which is derived from request.getRequestURI() and used as a URL prefix. These are all part of the same HTML branch in doGet that already lacks a Content-Type header (reported at line 116). The content itself consists of mostly static HTML template strings. While `consoleRoot` is user-influenced, it appears in href/src URL attributes only, not as raw HTML content. These are duplicate findings for the same root cause already reported at line 116, not separate vulnerabilities.

evidence.code_context: All println calls in lines 119-141 write static HTML strings. The only variable data is consoleRoot used in URL attributes. These are all within the same else branch of doGet that lacks Content-Type header, already reported as TP at line 116.
evidence.notes: The CodeQL query appears to flag each println/printf as a separate result but they are all part of the same single vulnerability instance: missing Content-Type in doGet HTML branch.
